item.title: 项目建议： | Project suggestions:
item.description:
费德费拉

发表于嗨，我目前正在做一个项目，我有一个大问题要问你。 我的项目唯一必须具备的条件是 Web 和桌面应用程序需要使用相同的代码。 此应用程序将仅在本地网络中运行（也可以是连接了许多主机的大型本地网络），它将有一个数据库（目前存储的少量数据）也需要是本地的，它将执行许多 API调用以从其他应用程序获取一些数据。 如果这个应用程序。 也可以为 Android 和 IOS 构建，这将是一个加号，但没有必要。 那么获得这项工作的最合适和更好的方法是什么？ 我会给你我的实际进展状态，也许从那开始你可以给我一些建议来改变一些东西或一切。

实际上我的项目分为两个文件夹：

  • 客户端：ReactJS + Redux + Axios + Bootstrap + Typescript
  • 服务器：NodeJS + Express + Prisma + Nodemon + Typescript
  • 数据库：MySQL

客户端是使用 CRA 命令创建的，而不是这些是我的问题：

  1. 目前在我的服务器端，我看到 Prisma 并不完全支持 ES6 模块，但是有一种解决方法正在运行，你是否建议我使用该解决方法进行生产或不使用 type: module 在我的package.json 并使用 ES5？

  2. 即使需要将相同的代码构建为桌面应用程序，将服务器与客户端分开是一种好方法吗？

  3. 我看到 ElectronJS 可以将我的应用程序构建为桌面应用程序。 你建议我在这种情况下使用它吗？ 如果是，您建议我如何包装我的代码以便将其构建为桌面应用程序。仅包装客户端或服务器和客户端？ 我的实际数据库是 MySQL，您建议我使用另一个数据库还是保留这个数据库来存储少量数据（数据很敏感，需要保护）？

Maniruzzaman Akash - Maniruzzaman Akash - Apr 22 4月22日



原文:

Fede-Ferra

Posted on Apr 26

Hi, i'm currently working on a project and i've got a big question for you. The only must have for my project is that the same code need to be used for both Web and a Desktop application. This application will run only in local networks (can be also be a big local network with many hosts connected), it will have a DB (small amount of data stored for now) that will also need to be local and it will do many API calls to fetch some datas from other apps. If this app. could also be built for Android and IOS it would be a plus but it is not necessary. So what is the most suitable and better way to get this work? I'll give you my actual state of progression and maybe starting from that you can give me some suggestions to change something or everything.

Actually my project is divided in two folders:

  • client: ReactJS + Redux + Axios + Bootstrap + Typescript
  • server: NodeJS + Express + Prisma + Nodemon + Typescript
  • DB: MySQL

The client is create with CRA command instead the These are my questions:

  1. Currently in my server side i saw that the ES6 module is not totally supported by Prisma but there is a workaround that is working, should you suggest me to go to production with that work around or to don't use the type: module in my package.json and work with ES5?

  2. Is it a good way of doing to separate the server from the client side even if the same code it needs to be built as a desktop application?

  3. I saw that ElectronJS can do the work of building my application as a desktop app. do you suggest me to use it in this case? if yes, how do you suggest me to wrap my code in order to built it as a desktop app., to wrap only the client or both server and client side? (if you can also link a guid to wrap my app. it would be appreciated.)

  4. My actual DB is a MySQL would you suggest me to use another one or to keep this one to store a small amount of datas (datas are sensitive and needs to be protected)?

Maniruzzaman Akash - Apr 22
item.author: eonin

item.title: 乌姆布拉卡达布拉！ Defence Against the Dark Arts of Magic Strings in Umbraco Umbraco 中魔法弦的黑魔法防御术 | Umbracadabra! Defence Against the Dark Arts of Magic Strings in Umbraco
item.description:
我敢肯定，在我们的职业生涯中，我们中的许多人都曾被告知“魔术弦很糟糕”，但究竟为什么会这样，又会出现什么问题呢？ 还有什么替代方法可以改进我们的代码？

为什么魔线这么差？

我们使用短语“magic strings”（以及在较小程度上的“magic numbers”）来指代直接在您的代码中使用的常量字符串。 作为 Umbraco 开发人员，我们可能非常熟悉的一个示例是Model.GetPropertyValue("bodyText") 。 这里， "bodyText"是魔术字符串。 文本可以在多个地方使用（可能在元标记以及页面正文中，或在不同的模板中），必须采用这种确切的格式（我不能将其替换为"Can I have the text for the body please?" ，尽管如此礼貌）并且指的是外部实体（在这种情况下是通过 Umbraco API 的 Umbraco 数据库）。 这些东西使它成为需要替换的魔术字符串的特别代码异味示例。

魔术字符串是一个术语，用于涵盖使用字符串代替更强大的方法的多种情况。

错字：魔法弦的大敌。 如果我们有一个特别高效的好莱坞式的下午，敲打那些键，你会因为误击奇怪的键而被原谅。

它们也可能特别难以发现：根据您的字体， "bodyIext"很容易被忽视。 编写我们的 IDE 的人都知道这一点，并会在可能的情况下纠正我们。 但是，字符串不能自动更正，因为 string 的任何值都是有效的 string 。 据 IDE 所知，您的意思是输入"bossyTRex" 。 这些会导致运行时错误，而不是更容易发现的编译时问题。

撇开胖手指不谈，魔术弦可能是那些说不同第一语言的人或阅读和写作有困难的人独有的。 即使您说相同的语言，在尝试同意如何拼写“颜色”时也会出现问题！

或者设置this.ValidationMethod = "none"的一段逻辑怎么样。 然后我们决定我们需要更改验证方法，但其他可能的值是什么并不明显。 魔术字符串不是自记录的，因此我们依赖于参考文档或“有一个注释解释了如何在某处使用它”或“我没有把它放在 README 文件中”。 这远非理想。

如果这还不够的话，与生产环境相比，魔术字符串会使替换值、测试代码或更改测试环境中的逻辑变得更加困难。 我们可以做得更好！

有没有麻瓜绳之类的东西？

是否存在字符串非魔术的情况？ 或者也许当一个魔术字符串是好的时候？ 是的，不是所有的字符串都是魔术字符串，也不是所有的魔术字符串都一定是坏的。

如果您在代码中输入字符串值，您甚至会在博客中编写代码吗，兄弟？ 将其归结为您需要问自己的 4 个问题：

  • 你可以重用这个常量吗？
  • 您可以一次轻松地更改几个值吗？
  • 它提高了可读性吗？
  • 它是配置的一部分吗？

这不会涵盖所有场景，但它是一个很好的起点。 如果您对这些问题的任何回答都是“是”，那么是时候寻找替代方案了。

您是否也随便放弃了“魔术数字”一词？ 我才刚刚掌握了魔线！

是啊。 对于那个很抱歉！

是的，魔术数字是魔术字符串的兄弟姐妹。 但它们可能会导致相同类型的问题。

以这段代码为例：

 public void PrepareForCheckout() { if(Customer.Country == "United Kingdom") { FinalValue = Value * 1.175; } }

这个 1.175 是什么还不是很明显。 您可能已经知道这是英国 17.5% 的旧税率，但很容易被忽略，尤其是非英国开发商。

既然我们知道这是增值税税率，我们也知道这是错误的。 几年前，英国将增值税税率改为 20%。 更正这个值也可能很困难——我们需要替换所有实例，但如果它写成value = value + value * (17.5/100) （有人以一种奇怪的方式处理代码清晰度！），一个简单的 find-and -替换是不够的。

例如，如果我们在某个地方有一个从任何地方引用的这个值的常量，那么代码在做什么会更清楚，也更容易更新。

 public void PrepareForCheckout() { if(Customer.Country == "United Kingdom") { FinalValue = Value * UkVatRate; } }

现在，我们可以对这段代码进行许多其他调整，但我刚刚解决了我们一直在谈论的那个。

我在本文中给出的第一个示例是Model.GetPropertyValue("bodyText") ，早在 2013 年的 Umbraco 7 天， Stephan Gay aka ZpqrtBnk 就发现它存在问题并开发了ModelsBuilder包。 通过为每种文档类型生成模型，它在跨我们的 Umbraco 解决方案与 IPublishedContent 交互时消除了对魔术字符串的需求。 一个持续的问题

我刚才提到了创建一个常量。 这可能是避免魔术字符串的最简单方法之一。 根据项目的大小和复杂性，它可以像将字符串拉到类中的常量声明一样简单。

 public class Cart { private const string BodyTextKey = "bodyText"; private const decimal UkVatRate = 1.175; //... }

在 .NET 中，常量（ const声明）实际上非常有效 - 在编译时，它们不会像变量那样分配任何内存，因此在性能方面直接使用每个位置的字符串是等效的。 但是，它确实通过删除魔术字符串帮助我们进行了很多预编译。

要使常量在您的项目中可重用，您可能会发现在您的项目中创建一个Constants.cs文件并将所有相关字符串粘贴在其中很有用。 这在较大的项目中可能会变得非常混乱，因此您可能需要进一步对常量进行分类，将它们分组到逻辑类和命名空间中，或者将常量添加到相关服务中，如下所示：

 public static class VatHelper { public const decimal UkVatRate = 1.175; //... }

事实上，如果我们将来需要向其他国家/地区申请增值税，我们可以更进一步：

 public static class VatHelper { public decimal GetVatRate(string country) { switch (country) { case Countries.UnitedKingdom: return 1.175; default: return 1; } } } //... public class Cart { private const string BodyTextKey = "bodyText"; private const decimal UkVatRate = 1.175; //... public void PrepareForCheckout() { FinalValue = FinalValue * VatHelper.GetVatRate(Customer.Country); } //... }

其他解决方案的枚举

常量很棒，但它们可能会有点重复。 枚举类型（对你我来说是枚举）对于有限数量的不会改变的常量很有用。

枚举是我们之前讨论的ValidationMethod设置的一个很好的解决方案。 我们可以创建一个包含所有可能值的枚举，并使用这个枚举代替我们的字符串：

 public enum ValidationMethod { None, Required, EmailAddress, PhoneNumber, Numeric } //... this.ValidationMethod = ValidationMethod.None; //... switch (field.ValidationMethod) { case ValidationMethod.Required: return !string.IsNullOrEmpty(field.Value); // ... }

枚举也适用于状态：

 public enum OrderStatus { Draft, Paid, Pending, Shipped, RecievedByCustomer } //... Order.Status = OrderStatus.Paid; //... if(Order.Status == OrderStatus.Paid) { ProcessOrder(); }

幻数和枚举

在幕后，枚举实际上为每个值存储整数。 对于我们的OrderStatus枚举， Draft相当于0 ， Paid是1 ， Pending是2等。您甚至可以显式设置应用于每个值的数字，如果您需要将可读名称映射到数字，这将很有用，或者如果您需要向现有枚举添加值。

 public enum OrderStatus { /// We want to add a new status at the top, /// but also to maintain the mapping of the original enum /// so we specify the values Cancelled = -1, Draft = 0, Paid = 1, Pending = 2, Shipped = 3, RecievedByCustomer = 4 } //... /// Readable definitions for all possible /// response codes from the ACME API docs public enum AcmeApiResponseCodes { Ok = 200, BadRequest = 400, Unauthorized = 401, InvalidClientId = 490, ExpiredClient = 491 }

作为基础值是整数的副作用，您可能会注意到，默认情况下，如果您将枚举作为 JSON 结果返回，您最终将返回数字而不是漂亮的枚举。

 { "orderNumber": 51138461315, "status": 2, "...": "..." }

所以我们这里有两个选择：返回数字，或者返回字符串。 如果您愿意的话，如果您想将枚举转换为字符串或从字符串转换，您有几个选项。

您可以使用简单的属性为您的单个属性设置转换器：

 public class Order { public long OrderNumber { get; set; } [JsonConverter(typeof(JsonStringEnumConverter))] public OrderStatus Status { get; set; } //... }

或者，如果您希望每次使用它时都将其序列化为字符串，请在枚举定义中设置属性 it。 当我们在这里时，您会注意到您还可以使用EnumMember属性自定义枚举如何呈现为字符串。

 [JsonConverter(typeof(JsonStringEnumConverter))] public enum OrderStatus { Draft, Paid, //... [EnumMember(Value = "Recieved by customer")] RecievedByCustomer }

或者最后，您可以通过修改Startup.cs中的ConfigureServices方法为所有枚举全局设置行为。 在调整默认 JSON 选项之前，我们需要添加AddMvcAndRazor以获取 MVC 配置。

 public void ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services) { services .AddUmbraco(_env, _config) // Here's the good stuff .AddMvcAndRazor(mvc => { mvc.AddJsonOptions(json => { json.JsonSerializerOptions.Converters.Add(new JsonStringEnumConverter()); }); }) // That's all, folks! .AddBackOffice() .AddWebsite() .AddComposers() .Build(); }

如果您在 Umbraco 9+/.NET 5+ 应用程序中使用 Umbraco 8 或更低版本（使用 .NET Framework）或使用Newtonsoft.Json （而不是System.Text.Json ），您的配置可能会略有不同，但原理是一样的。

标记的枚举

简单的枚举适用于状态之类的东西，在任何时候只有一个可以为真。 但是，多个值在哪里有意义呢？

我们之前的ValidationMethod示例在这里适用。 可能有一个字段需要验证为Required ，还有EmailAddress 。 对于字符串，我们可以将其作为一个数组来完成，如果我们愿意，我们也可以对枚举做同样的事情......

 // How we might have allowed multiple values with magic strings field.ValidationMethods = new string[] { "email address", "required" }; // We can do the same when using enums too field.ValidationMethods = new ValidationMethod[] { ValidationMethod.EmailAddress, ValidationMethod.Required };

但是我们也可以使用标记的枚举做得更好，以允许枚举具有多个值。

 [Flags] public enum ValidationMethod { None = 0, Required = 1, EmailAddress = 2, PhoneNumber = 4, Numeric = 8 } //... this.ValidationMethods = ValidationMethod.EmailAddress | ValidationMethod.Required; //... if(this.ValidationMethods.HasFlag(ValidationMethod.Required)) { return !string.IsNullOrEmpty(field.Value); }

在此示例中，您可以看到我们已将[Flags]属性添加到枚举并为每个枚举分配了一个值 - 这一点很重要。 然后，我们可以使用“按位或运算符”或管道字符（ | ）分配多个值，这与我们在 or 语句中使用两个 of （ this || that ）相同。

要检查枚举是否有标志，我们可以在枚举值上使用HasFlag 。 我不得不重构我们之前的 switch 语句，以便为我们要检查的每个值使用 if 语句。

您可能会注意到我分配给每个数字的整数不是按顺序排列的。 好吧，它是按顺序排列的，但是使用加倍序列：1、2、4、8... 等。每个数字都是前一个值的两倍。 这很重要，因为标记的枚举仍然只存储一个整数！

它通过使用按位运算（又是那个词）来工作，简单地说，就是查看二进制数的各个“位”（我的意思是在技术术语中！）并应用“或”运算 - 如果有的话该列中的位为 1，则返回 1，否则返回 0。

对于那些好奇的人，让我们看一下二进制加倍序列中的数字。

 0000 (0) 0001 (1) 0010 (2[\*](#footnote)) 0100 (4) 1000 (8) ...etc

你会注意到，因为它们都是 2 的幂，所以每列只包含一个 1。因此，无论我们在按位 OR 中对它们进行什么组合，我们都可以计算出哪些初始枚举值用于创建它。

 var a = ValidationMethod.EmailAddress | ValidationMethod.Required; /// That's 1 and 2 /// /// 0001 /// OR 0010 /// ------- /// 0011 (the last two columns have a 1 in the first number OR second number) var b = ValidationMethod.Required | ValidationMethod.EmailAddress | ValidationMethod.PhoneNumber| ValidationMethod.Numeric; /// That's 1 and 2 /// /// 0001 /// 0010 /// 0100 /// OR 1000 /// ------- /// 1111

在上面的两个示例中，要检查值是否有标志Required ，即0001 ，我们只需要检查最后一列中的1 - HasFlag方法实际上是在幕后使用按位与标志 ( & ) 来执行此操作查看。

 this.ValidationMethods.HasFlag(ValidationMethod.Required); // is the same as (this.ValidationMethods & ValidationMethod.Required) == ValidationMethod.Required; // is the same as // Binary literals in C# are prefixed with 0b (C# 7+) 0b0000011 & 0b0000001 == 0b0000001; /// 0011 /// AND 0001 /// -------- /// 0001 (the last column has a 1 in the first number AND second number)

由于这种行为，我们还可以通过将组合值的所有值加在一起来将常见组合的值或全部添加到我们的枚举中（在这种情况下，加法和按位运算提供相同的结果，因为它们都是 2 的幂，但不正常！）

 [Flags] public enum ValidationMethod { // Regular items None = 0, Required = 1, EmailAddress = 2, PhoneNumber = 4, Numeric = 8, //Combinations RequiredEmail = 3 // 0001 | 0010 = 0011 or cheat by doing 1 + 2 = 3 All = 15 // 0001 | 0010 | 0100 | 1000 = 1111 or cheat by doing 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 8 = 15 }

在这种情况下，并不是说“全部”值有任何意义，但很高兴知道！

配置你的出路

我们一开始问的一个问题是“它是配置的一部分吗？” 这是一个很好的问题，因为它很可能会改变我们处理它的方式。 什么是配置？ 我喜欢考虑任何可以改变应用程序行为的东西。 该值将（在规范中或您自己）被标记为可能会改变的东西——无论是在未来还是在每个环境中。 它不在您的应用程序的控制范围内，并且可能会发生变化

  • 或者您可能希望将您的登台站点指向 API 的沙盒版本。

    • 现在我们在 .NET 5 的土地上，我们甚至可以通过将整个配置部分映射到 C# 对象来摆脱我们过去用来从配置中提取值的一些魔术字符串。 我还使用了一个常量来获取配置部分的名称，以避免将其作为魔术字符串。

     public class AcmeConfig { public const string Section = "ACME"; public string ApiBaseUrl { get; set; } //... } //... public class AcmeClient { public AcmeClient(IConfiguration configuration) { var config = configuration.GetSection(AcmeConfig.Section).Get<AcmeConfig>(); //... } //... }

    乌姆布拉卡达布拉！ 无魔法代码

    希望我能够解释一下为什么可能需要避免使用这些魔法字符串，以及我们如何改进它们。 不要发疯 - 无需替换代码中的每个变量，但以后每次打开这些双引号时都值得考虑！

    快乐const -ing.. 和enum -ing... 和模型构建！

    * There are 10 types of people in the world: those who understand binary and those who don't. *世界上有10种人：懂二进制的和不懂二进制的。



    原文:

    I'm sure many of us have been told at some point in our careers that "magic strings are bad" but why exactly is that and what could go wrong? And what alternatives are there to improve our code?

    Why are magic strings so bad?

    We use the phrase "magic strings" (and, to a lesser extent, "magic numbers") to refer to a constant string used directly in your code. An example of this that we, as Umbraco developers, may be all too familiar with is Model.GetPropertyValue("bodyText"). Here, "bodyText" is the magic string. The text can be used in multiple places (maybe in the meta tags as well as the page body, or on different templates), has to be in this exact format (I can't swap it out with "Can I have the text for the body please?", as polite as that is) and refers to an external entity (the Umbraco database via the Umbraco APIs in this case). These things make it a particularly code-smelly example of a magic string that needs replacing.

    Magic strings is a term used to cover multiple scenarios of when a string is used in place of a more robust method.

    Typos: the arch enemy of magic strings. If we're having a particularly productive, Hollywood-hacker-style afternoon, smacking away at those keys, you'd be excused for mis-hitting the odd one.

    Clip from NCIS showing two people typing manically on one keyboard

    They can be particularly difficult to spot too: depending on your font, "bodyIext" could easily go unnoticed. The folks that write our IDE's know this, and will correct us where possible. A string, however, cannot be autocorrected because any value of string is a valid string. As far as the IDE knows, you meant to type "bossyTRex". These cause runtime errors rather than the easier-to-spot compile-time issues.

    Fat-fingered-ness aside, magic strings can be exclusive to those who speak a different first language or people who have difficulty reading and writing. Even if you speak the same language issues can arise when trying to agree how to spell "colour"!

    Or how about a piece of logic that sets this.ValidationMethod = "none". We then later decide we need to change the validation method, but it's not obvious what the other possible values are. Magic strings aren't self-documenting and so we rely on reference documents or "there's a comment that explains how to use it somewhere" or "didn't I put that in the README file". This is far from ideal.

    And if all that wasn't enough, magic strings make it more difficult to replace a value, test your code or change logic on your test environments compared to production. We can do better!

    Is there such thing as a muggle string?

    Are there cases when a string is non-magic? Or perhaps when a magic string is ok? Yes, not all strings are magic strings and not all magic strings are nescessarily bad.

    If you're typing a string value in code, the blog do you even code, bro? has boiled it down to 4 questions you need to ask yourself:

    • Can you reuse this constant?
    • Can you easily change a few values at once?
    • Does it improve readability?
    • Is it part of configuration?

    This won't cover all scenarios, but it's a good place to get started. If any of your answers to these questions is "yes", it's time to look into alternatives.

    Did you just casually drop the term "magic numbers" too? I've only just got to grips with magic strings!

    Ah, yes. Sorry about that!

    Yes, magic numbers are the lesser talked about sibling of magic strings. But they can cause the same sorts of issues.

    Take this code as an example:

    public void PrepareForCheckout() {
    if(Customer.Country == "United Kingdom") {
        FinalValue = Value * 1.175;
    }
}

    It's not immediately obvious what this 1.175 is. You might have picked up that it's the old tax rate in the UK of 17.5%, but it could easily be missed, especially by a non-British developer.

    Now that we know this is the VAT rate, we also know it's wrong. The UK changed their VAT rate to 20% a few years back. Correcting this value could be difficult too - we need to replace all instances, but if it's written as value = value + value * (17.5/100) (someone has gone about code clarity in an odd way!), a simple find-and-replace won't suffice.

    If, for example, we had a constant of this value somewhere that we reference from everywhere, it would be both clearer what the code was doing and easier to update.

    public void PrepareForCheckout() {
    if(Customer.Country == "United Kingdom") {
        FinalValue = Value * UkVatRate;
    }
}

    Now, there are many other tweaks we could make to this code, but I've just solved the one we've been talking about.

    ModelsBuilder to the rescue

    The first example I gave in this article was Model.GetPropertyValue("bodyText"), which, back in the Umbraco 7-days of 2013, Stephan Gay aka ZpqrtBnk identified as a problem and developed the ModelsBuilder package. ModelsBuilder is now a part of Umbraco and if you're not using already, you really should be. It removes the need for magic strings when interacting with IPublishedContent across our Umbraco solutions by generating models for each document type. Dave Woestenborghs wrote an article on Getting started with Umbraco Modelsbuilder back in 2016 which still holds up well.

    A constant problem

    I mentioned creating a constant just now. And that's probably one of the simplest methods of avoiding magic strings. Depending on the size and complexity of your project, it can be as simple as pulling your strings up to constant declarations in your class.

    public class Cart {
    private const string BodyTextKey = "bodyText";
    private const decimal UkVatRate = 1.175;

    //...
}

    In .NET, constants (const declarations) are actually pretty efficient - at compile-time, they're not allocated any memory like variables are, so it's equivalent performance-wise to directly using the string in each location. It does, however, help us out a lot pre-compilation by removing the magic strings.

    To make constants reusable across your project you might find it useful to create a Constants.cs file in your project and stick all your relevant strings in there. This can get pretty cluttered on bigger projects, so you may need to categorise your constants further, grouping them into logical classes and namespaces or adding your constants to related services like so:

    public static class VatHelper {
    public const decimal UkVatRate = 1.175;

    //...
}

    In fact, with a helper, we could go even further in case we need to apply VAT to other countries in the future:

    public static class VatHelper {
    public decimal GetVatRate(string country) {
        switch (country) {
            case Countries.UnitedKingdom: 
                return 1.175;
            default:
                return 1;       
        }
    }
}

//...

public class Cart {
    private const string BodyTextKey = "bodyText";
    private const decimal UkVatRate = 1.175;

    //...

    public void PrepareForCheckout() {
        FinalValue = FinalValue * VatHelper.GetVatRate(Customer.Country);
    }

    //...
}

    (OK, so in most real-world examples the country and it's respective VAT rate would probably both live in a database somewhere, but it works as an example!)

    Enum-ber of other solutions

    Constants are great and all, but they can get a little bit repetitive. Enumerated types (that's enums to you and me) are useful for a finite number of constants that won't change.

    Enums are a great solution to our ValidationMethod setting we were talking about earlier. We could create an enum with all the possible values and use this enum in place of our strings:

    public enum ValidationMethod {
    None,
    Required,
    EmailAddress,
    PhoneNumber,
    Numeric
}

//...

this.ValidationMethod = ValidationMethod.None;

//...

switch (field.ValidationMethod) {
    case ValidationMethod.Required:
        return !string.IsNullOrEmpty(field.Value);
        // ...
}

    Enums also work well for statuses:

    public enum OrderStatus {
    Draft,
    Paid,
    Pending,
    Shipped,
    RecievedByCustomer
}

//...

Order.Status = OrderStatus.Paid;

//...

if(Order.Status == OrderStatus.Paid) {
    ProcessOrder();
}

    Magic numbers and enums

    Under the covers, enums actually store integers for each value. In the case of our OrderStatus enum, Draft is equivalent to 0, Paid is 1, Pending is 2, etc. You can even explicitly set the number applied to each value which can be useful if you need to map a readable name to a number, or if you need to add a value to an existing enum.

    public enum OrderStatus {

    /// We want to add a new status at the top,
    /// but also to maintain the mapping of the original enum
    /// so we specify the values

    Cancelled = -1,
    Draft = 0,
    Paid = 1,
    Pending = 2,
    Shipped = 3,
    RecievedByCustomer = 4
}

//...

/// Readable definitions for all possible
/// response codes from the ACME API docs
public enum AcmeApiResponseCodes {
    Ok = 200,
    BadRequest = 400,
    Unauthorized = 401,
    InvalidClientId = 490,
    ExpiredClient = 491
}

    JSON and enums

    As a side effect of the underlying value being an integer, you might notice that by default if you return an enum as a JSON result, you'll end up returning the number rather than the pretty enum.

    {
    "orderNumber": 51138461315,
    "status": 2,

    "...": "..."
}

    JSON (and JavaScript too, without some workarounds) don't support enums. So we have two choices here: return the number, or return the string. You've got a few options if you want to convert the enum to and from a string if you'd rather.

    You can set the converter for your individual property with a simple attribute:

    public class Order {

    public long OrderNumber { get; set; }

    [JsonConverter(typeof(JsonStringEnumConverter))]
    public OrderStatus Status { get; set; }

    //...
}

    Alternatively, set the attribute it on the enum definition if you want it to be serialized to a string every time you use it. While we're here, you'll notice you can also customise how the enum is rendered as a string with the EnumMember attribute.

    [JsonConverter(typeof(JsonStringEnumConverter))]  
public enum OrderStatus {
    Draft,
    Paid,

    //...

    [EnumMember(Value = "Recieved by customer")]
    RecievedByCustomer
}

    Or finally, you can set the behaviour globally for all enums by modifying the ConfigureServices method in Startup.cs. We need to add AddMvcAndRazor to get ahold of the MVC config before adjusting the default JSON options.

    public void ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services)
{
    services
        .AddUmbraco(_env, _config)

        // Here's the good stuff

        .AddMvcAndRazor(mvc =>
        {
            mvc.AddJsonOptions(json =>
            {
                json.JsonSerializerOptions.Converters.Add(new JsonStringEnumConverter());
            });
        })

        // That's all, folks!

        .AddBackOffice()
        .AddWebsite()
        .AddComposers()
        .Build();
}

    Your configuration may differ slightly if you're using Umbraco 8 or below (with .NET Framework) or if you're using Newtonsoft.Json (rather than System.Text.Json) in your Umbraco 9+/.NET 5+ app, but the principles are the same.

    Flagged enums

    Simple enums work fine for things like statuses, where only one can be true at any one time. But how about where multiple values make sense?

    Our ValidationMethod example from earlier applies here. It may be possible to have a field that needs validating as Required but also EmailAddress. With strings, we could have done this as an array and we can do the same with enums if we want...

    // How we might have allowed multiple values with magic strings
field.ValidationMethods = new string[] { "email address", "required" };

// We can do the same when using enums too
field.ValidationMethods = new ValidationMethod[] { ValidationMethod.EmailAddress, ValidationMethod.Required };

    But we can also do one better with flagged enums to allow an enum to have multiple values.

    [Flags]
public enum ValidationMethod {
    None = 0,
    Required = 1,
    EmailAddress = 2,
    PhoneNumber = 4,
    Numeric = 8
}

//...

this.ValidationMethods = ValidationMethod.EmailAddress | ValidationMethod.Required;

//...

if(this.ValidationMethods.HasFlag(ValidationMethod.Required)) {
        return !string.IsNullOrEmpty(field.Value);
}

    In this example, you can see we've added the [Flags] attribute to the enum and assigned each enum a value - this bit is important. We can then assign multiple values using the "bitwise OR operator" or pipe character (|) it's the same we use two of in an or statement ( this || that).

    To check if an enum has a flag, we can use the HasFlag on the enum value. I've had to refactor our switch statement from earlier to use an if statement for each value we want to check for.

    You might notice the integer I've assigned to each number isn't in order. Well, it is in order, but using a doubling sequence: 1, 2, 4, 8... etc. Each number is double the previous value. This is important because a flagged enum still only stores one integer!

    It works by using bitwise operations (there's that word again) which, simply put, is looking at the individual "bits" (and I mean that in the technical term!) of a binary number and applying an "OR" operation - if any of the bits in that column is 1, it returns a 1, otherwise it returns a 0.

    For those curious, let's look at the numbers in the doubling sequence in binary.

    
0000 (0)
0001 (1)
0010 (2[\*](#footnote))
0100 (4)
1000 (8)

...etc

    You'll notice, that because they're all powers of 2, each column only ever contains a single 1. Therefore, no matter what combination we make of these in a bitwise OR, we can work out which initial enum values went into creating it.

    var a = ValidationMethod.EmailAddress | ValidationMethod.Required;
/// That's 1 and 2
///
/// 0001
/// OR 0010
/// -------
/// 0011 (the last two columns have a 1 in the first number OR second number)

var b = ValidationMethod.Required | ValidationMethod.EmailAddress | ValidationMethod.PhoneNumber| ValidationMethod.Numeric;
/// That's 1 and 2
///
/// 0001
/// 0010
/// 0100
/// OR 1000
/// -------
/// 1111

    In both examples above, to check the value has the flag Required, which is 0001, we simply need to check for a 1 in the last column - the HasFlag method is actually using the bitwise AND flag (&) under the covers which does this check.

    this.ValidationMethods.HasFlag(ValidationMethod.Required);

// is the same as

(this.ValidationMethods & ValidationMethod.Required) == ValidationMethod.Required;

// is the same as

// Binary literals in C# are prefixed with 0b (C# 7+)
0b0000011 & 0b0000001 == 0b0000001;

/// 0011
/// AND 0001
/// --------
/// 0001 (the last column has a 1 in the first number AND second number)

    Because of this behaviour, we can also add a value for common combinations or all into our enum by adding all the values of the combining values together (adding and bitwise operations provide the same result in this case because they're all powers of two, but not normally!)

    [Flags]
public enum ValidationMethod {
    // Regular items
    None = 0,
    Required = 1,
    EmailAddress = 2,
    PhoneNumber = 4,
    Numeric = 8,

    //Combinations
    RequiredEmail = 3 // 0001 | 0010 = 0011 or cheat by doing 1 + 2 = 3
    All = 15 // 0001 | 0010 | 0100 | 1000 = 1111 or cheat by doing 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 8 = 15
}

    Not that an "All" value makes any sense in this case, but it's good to know!

    Config your way out of it

    One of the questions we asked at the beginning was "is it part of configuration?" This is a good question to ask because it may well change how we deal with it. What is configuration? I like to think of anything that can alter an application's behaviour. This value will have been flagged (in a specification or by yourself) as something that's likely to change - be that in the future or per environment. A URL to an API endpoint is a good example of a configuration variable:

    • it sits outside the control of your application and could conceivably change
    • or you may want to point your staging site at a sandbox version of the API.

    Now we're in the land of .NET 5, we can even get rid of some of the magic strings we have historically used to pull values out of config by mapping whole configuration sections to C# objects. I've also used a constant to get the configuration section name, to avoid that as a magic string.

    public class AcmeConfig
{
    public const string Section = "ACME";

    public string ApiBaseUrl { get; set; }

    //...
}

//...

public class AcmeClient {
    public AcmeClient(IConfiguration configuration) {
        var config = configuration.GetSection(AcmeConfig.Section).Get<AcmeConfig>();

        //...
    }

    //...
}

    Umbracadabra! Magic-less code

    Hopefully I've been able to explain a little of why avoiding these magic strings might be necessary and how we can improve upon them. Don't go crazy - there's no need to replace every variable in your code, but its worth thinking about in the future each time you open those double-quotes!

    Happy const-ing.. and enum-ing... and Model Building!

    *There are 10 types of people in the world: those who understand binary and those who don't.
item.author: joeglombek

item.title: 你为什么对它如此痴迷？ | Why are you being such a git about it?
item.description:
你的 git repos 是代码的垃圾场吗？ 我对人们如何使用 git 真的很挑剔——这是一件好事。 我们将看看分支策略、提交消息的重要性以及您应该多久提交一次？ 让我们研究一下如何通过 5 个简单的技巧将代码转储变成一个光荣的软件存档，以及如何节省时间、挫折和金钱。

让我们开始非常简单：什么是 git？ 它可以确保您的代码得到备份、版本控制并可供其他开发人员访问。 然而，如果使用得当，它还可以发挥更多作用——帮助简化复杂的流程，例如发布管理、功能开发和多人同时工作。

不过，我可以对它有点 git：我对人们如何使用 git 真的很挑剔——这是一件好事。 多年来，我与相当多的组织和开发团队合作过，git 利用率低下是一个比你想象的更常见的问题。 人们将 git 用作代码的垃圾场，而不是管理良好的软件存档。 今天我们来看看 5 个简单的技巧，如果运气好的话，它们会改善你的 git 练习。

为什么开发人员不想使用 git？

他们害怕承诺

这不是 5 个之一，但确实有一些组织还没有使用 SCM。 由于我之前给出的所有原因，SCM 对于现代软件开发至关重要。

至于 git 客户端，那里有很多。 有些人喜欢使用命令行——这很好……如果你是一个 git pro！ 但为什么过于复杂？ 有时，即使对于自信的 git 用户来说，能够以树的形式可视化提交历史也可以成为救命稻草。 我最喜欢的树可视化（因此也是最喜欢的整体 git 客户端）是GitKraken（这是一个推荐链接 - 我想要一件 GitKraken T 恤！） 。 它对开源项目是免费的，但您需要为私有存储库付费。 我将在本文中使用 GitKraken 作为示例，因为它是我最熟悉的客户端 - 但你可以使用任何你喜欢的客户端，但我强烈建议不要使用剥离客户端，例如 Visual Studio 或 GitHub Desktop当您进入更复杂的 git 使用和流程时。 如果 GitKraken 不是你的包，你可能想研究Fork（也付费，可用于 Mac 和 Windows） 、 Git Extensions或者至少是Oh My Posh 和 Windows Terminal 。

这部xkcd漫画完美地突出了这个问题。

我想说这只是漫画中的一个笑话，但它可能太真实了......

调查问题并扫描 git 历史记录中有用的提交消息.. 😫

想象一下处于丹尼斯的情况，并且：

  • 您发现了一个错误，但找不到可能发生的提交
  • 你在代码库中看到一些奇怪的东西，没有评论，所以你使用责备/注释工具来查看谁更改了它以及为什么......但是安迪正在度假，他的提交消息说“测试”😱
  • 自从您上次看到它以来，某些代码已经更改 - 为什么？ 你可以把它恢复到你喜欢的样子，但也许它是出于某种原因而改变的？ 你再次启动了责备工具，“修复 Chrome 中的日期格式问题”——最好保持原样！ 由良好的提交消息保存！

有些人会告诉你，好的提交信息应该被写成好像在回答“这个提交做什么？”这个问题。 这是个好建议！ 使用诸如“Fixes ...”、“Reverts ...”或“Integrates ...”之类的动词开始您的提交消息也是一个好习惯。

但仅这些是不够的。 详细，详细，详细！

最好保持标题简短（GitKraken 建议使用 72 个字符），但您可以在描述字段中添加更多详细信息。

您甚至可以在命令行上执行此操作： git commit -m "Title" -m "Description"

修复 Chrome 中的日期格式问题

无论浏览器设置为何种语言环境，Chrome 始终采用 MM/DD 格式*

恢复新的“Paytastic Checkout”结账流程

客户改变主意并希望恢复使用 LegacyCart

集成短信 API

使用“Simple SMS Sender”API 在订单发货时向客户发送 SMS

如果您有票务系统或待办事项列表，有时在您的提交中包含项目的标题会有所帮助，并且 ID 或指向初始问题的链接可能会非常有用。

关于人们承诺的频率，我见过很多不同的策略，但我听到的最有说服力的论点是“很少而且经常”。 不要忘记 git 充当您工作和进度的备份。 仅在功能完成时才提交是没有意义的：如果您的硬盘驱动器在一夜之间打包会发生什么？ 或者你明天病了，剩下的人来接你的工作？ 提交部分完成的工作比不提交要有用得多。 虽然确保每个提交都处于可构建状态通常是一种很好的做法 - 如果您的工作进行中 (WIP) 提交没有构建，那也没关系。

您还可以使用微小的提交来帮助您恢复不需要的功能，而从更大的提交中提取小块代码可能会很痛苦。

也许将配色方案更改为红色背景上的绿色文本有点……？ 将“将颜色方案更改为红色上的绿色”与“重新排序幻灯片”捆绑在一起太容易了，但是将它们分开可以更容易地在以后恢复其中一个。

但是，所有这些“很少而且经常”的提交不是在一段时间后开始引起轰动吗？ 但是，明天早上来，我已经完成了那一小部分工作，我不想要两次提交“WIP - Restyle footer”和“Restyle footer”。 所以我可以修改我的“WIP”提交以包含所有更改并将其重命名为“Restyle footer”。

或者在命令行中git commit --amend -m "Restyle footer" 。 如果您想合并多个提交，或者您已经提交了第二轮更改，您可以查看“压缩”提交。

在 GitKraken 中，这是通过选择要组合的多个提交，右键单击并选择“Squash”来完成的。 在某些情况下，无法进行压缩 - 在这种情况下，您最好将历史记录原样保留！

如果我在家里做一些 DIY，周围的工具和污垢会变得很乱。 在我睡觉之前，我可能会快速收拾一下，但是第二天，一旦我完成了这项工作，所有的工具都会消失，我会用吸尘器清理我弄得一团糟。 在我让其他人进屋之前，我一定会把它打扫干净！ 以同样的方式对待你的树枝。 当你正在处理事情时，它们有点乱是可以的，但是一旦你完成或准备合并到一个共享分支中，你应该整理它 - 没有人想看到你凌乱的分支！

不过要注意一件事：不要压制你的错误！ 你的错误和返工讲述了一个故事。 它可以为未来的开发人员提供有价值的解释，并记录你的错误和学习。 使用修正和挤压来整理，而不是扫在地毯下！

那里有很多分支策略，我真的不介意你使用哪一个。 我个人最喜欢的是GitHub flow和Common Flow （或两者的组合），但还有更多。 通常，它们遵循功能驱动的开发模式，每个功能都有一个分支，以及特定版本的一些状态概念（通常使用分支或标签）。

我还想确保一次只有一个人致力于任何一个分支 - 如果两个人正在开发一个功能，则将该功能分解为子功能。 这是我的“混乱的正在进行的分支”方法起作用的唯一方法！

在部署更改以供测试之前，您与同事讨论并审查您的代码更改。 一旦每个人都满意，您的 Pull Request 就会合并到main分支中。 值得注意的是，如果这些 git 流对您来说都没有意义，您可以从中获取灵感。 我不知道有多少公司严格遵循任何一种特定策略。 因此，选择一个作为基准并根据您的组织对其进行调整。

好的，所以你已经有了你的分支策略并且你已经完成了你的功能——我们准备好合并了！

但是在具有多个并发工作流程的大型项目中，您最终可能会遇到真正纠结的分支和合并。

那么合并这些功能还有哪些其他选择呢？

创建一个合并提交（这会造成您在上面看到的混乱）

  • 挤压和合并
  • 变基和合并

    • 现在，有些人喜欢压缩和变基：你的整个功能被压缩到一个提交中，然后直接放到开发或主分支上——这是 GitHub 的“压缩和合并”的一种极端替代方案。 这很整洁，我给你！ 但是，您会丢失很多关于何时、如何以及为什么进行代码更改的文档。 我宁愿尽可能地保留这段历史。

    这给我们留下了“变基和合并”。 这个选项允许我们在保持树干净整洁的同时避免丢失数据，当然，这是我的最爱。 变基是在目标分支（在本例中为main或 development）之上的分支（我们的功能分支）中“重放”每个提交的行为，就好像它们develop在这个较新的时间点一样。

    如您所见，这种方法消除了交叉合并的混乱，同时保持了整个分支结构和每次提交中的所有细节。 每个功能都与main分支或develop分支形成整洁的“D”形，没有交叉。

    您也可以在没有拉取请求的情况下执行此过程，利用各个工具将您的功能重新定位到main上，然后在您选择的任何rebase客户端中将您的功能merge到main中。

    关于改写历史的说明

    技巧 4 和 5 都在 git 世界中做了一些有争议的事情：重写历史。 不是像粉饰历史暴行那样“重写历史”，而只是在发生（重写）之后更改推送到 git 存储库（AKA 历史）的内容。 压缩、修改、变基和打补丁都是 git 历史被重写的例子。

    我一直在积极倡导其中的 3 个！

    维护你的“真实历史”当然有它的优点：它对学习和代码审查很有用。 如果你从不篡改它，也不可能搞砸你的历史！

    但是，我认为这不应该以您的回购的可读性为代价。 我更喜欢保持我喜欢称之为“功能历史”的东西，其中我们的功能开发顺序是正确的，即使我们在每个功能相对于彼此的开发时稍微调整了时间线。

    我不主张完全重写，只是快速整理一下。

    至于我在重写历史时提到的那个“风险”，只要确保你在开始弄乱历史之前已经推送了你的存储库。 然后在你的力量推动之前仔细检查。

    从代码转储到令人惊叹的软件存档

    就是这样。 这是我们令人惊叹的软件档案。 我们通过小的增量提交进行源代码控制，每个都有一个有意义且详细的提交消息，舒适地放置在功能分支中，没有分散注意力的噪音和复杂的合并。 它从下到上轻松阅读，没有隐藏细节或掩盖错误。

    干净的历史允许功能轻松回滚，而详细的提交消息可以更好地理解存储库历史，从而减少错误，分支策略可以明确在任何时间点将哪些代码部署到哪个环境。 现在可以清楚地看到 git 纪律如何为开发人员、经理和最终客户带来好处——为所有参与项目的人节省时间、挫折和金钱。



    原文:

    Are your git repos a dumping ground for code? I'm really fussy about how people use git - and that's a good thing. We'll take a look at branching strategies, the importance of commit messages and how often should you commit, anyway? Let's investigate how, with 5 simple tips, we can turn a code dump into a glorious archive of software and how this can save time, frustration and money.

    Let's start really simple: what is git? Git is the most popular Source Control Management (SCM) system. It can ensure your code is backed up, versioned and accessible to other developers. Used properly, however, it can be so much more - helping ease complex processes such as release management, feature development and multiple people working concurrently.

    I can, though, be a bit of a git about it: I'm really fussy about how people use git - and that's a good thing. I've worked with a fair few organisations and development teams over the years, and poor git utilisation is a more common issue than you might think. People use git as a dumping ground for code rather than a well-managed archive of software. Today we'll take a look at 5 simple tips which will, with any luck, improve your git practice.

    0. Source Control yourselves

    Why did the developer not want to use git?

    They were scared of committing to it

    This isn't one of the 5, but there are seriously some organisations out there not using SCM yet. SCM is not the same as a backup and if you've emailed some code to a colleague, you're probably doing it wrong!

    For all the reasons I gave previously, SCM is essential for modern day software development.

    Mercurial (hg), Team Foundation Version Control (TFVC) and Subversion (SVN) are all examples of SCMs but Git has really taken over in the software world and is the industry standard - I'd recommend it for that on its own: there's no point teaching your team a SCM system which is (or may become) redundant.

    As for git clients, there are so many out there. Some like to use the command line - and that's great... if you're a git pro! But why overcomplicate? And there are times when being able to visualise the commit history in tree form can be a life-saver even for confident git-users. My favourite tree visualisation (and therefore favourite overall git client) is GitKraken (this is a referral link - I want a GitKraken t-shirt!). It's free for open source projects, but you'll need to pay for private repositories. I'll be using GitKraken for examples in this article as it's the client I'm most familiar with - but you can use any client you like but I'd strongly recommend not using a stripped-back client such as Visual Studio or GitHub Desktop as you get into more complex git usage and processes. If GitKraken isn't your bag, you may want to investigate Fork (also paid, available for Mac and Windows), Git Extensions or, at the very least, Oh My Posh and Windows Terminal.

    1. Commit Messaging is everything

    This xkcd comic highlights the problem perfectly.

    I'd like to say that was just a joke in a comic strip, but it can be all too real...

    Investigating an issue and by scanning through useful commit messages in git history.. 😫

    Imagine being in Dennis' situation and:

    • you've discovered a bug and you can't find the commit where it likely happened
    • you see something odd in the codebase, there's no comment so you use a blame/annotate tool to see who changed it and why... but Andy is on holiday and his commit message says "test" 😱
    • some piece of code has changed since you last saw it - why? You could revert it back to how you like it but maybe it was changed for a reason? You fire up the blame tool again, "Fixes date formatting issue in Chrome" - better leave it as it is! Saved by a good commit message!

    Some people will tell you that good commit messages should be written as if answering the question "what does this commit do?" and that's good advice! Starting your commit message with a verb like "Fixes ...", "Reverts ..." or "Integrates ..." is a good practice too.

    But these alone are not enough. "Fixes bugs" or "Reverts broken changes" are still useless. Detail, detail, detail!

    GitKraken (and many other GUI git clients) give you two fields for your commit messages - a title and a description. It's best to keep your title short (GitKraken recommends 72 characters) but you can add even more detail in the description field.

    You can even do this on the command line: git commit -m "Title" -m "Description"

    Fixes date formatting issue in Chrome

    Chrome always assumes the MM/DD format no matter what locale the browser is set to*

    *it doesn't, this is just a made up example

    Reverts new "Paytastic Checkout" checkout flow

    Customer has changed their mind and wants to revert to using LegacyCart

    Integrates SMS API

    Sending an SMS to customers when their order ships using the "Simple SMS Sender" API

    If you have a ticketing system or to-do list, it sometimes helps to include the title of the item in your commit and an ID or link to the initial issue can prove very useful.

    GitKraken (and many other GUI git clients) let you hook up your issue tracker to your git repos so you can automatically stick an issue number into the branch name (and it'll therefore be in the merge commit too!)

    2. Push Little and often

    I've seen a lot of different tactics when it comes to how often people commit, but the most persuasive argument I've heard is for "little and often". Don't forget git acts as a backup of your work and progress. There's no point only committing when something is feature complete: what happens if your hard drive packs up overnight? Or you're off sick tomorrow and somebody else is left to pick up your work? It's far more useful to have partially complete work committed than not. Although it's generally good practice to ensure each commit is in a buildable state - it's ok if your work-in-progress (WIP) commits don't build.

    You can also use your tiny commits to help you revert unwanted pieces of functionality, while it can be a real pain to pull small chunks of code out of a bigger commit.

    Perhaps changing the colour-scheme to green text on a red background was a bit much...? It would have been all too easy to bundle "Change colour-scheme to green on red" in with "Reorder slides", but separating these out made it a lot easier to revert one of them at a later date.

    3. Squash rackets

    But don't all these "little and often" commits start to make a racket after a while? (See what I did there?) That's where squash and amend come in. As I mentioned, I'm a big fan of a WIP (Work -In-Progress) commit at the end of the day. But, come tomorrow morning and I've finished that small segment of work off, I don't want two commits "WIP - Restyle footer" and "Restyle footer". So I can amend my "WIP" commit to include all changes and rename it to "Restyle footer".

    Or in the command line git commit --amend -m "Restyle footer". Atlassian has a great tutorial about rewriting git history from the command line.

    If you want to combine multiple commits, or you've already committed your second round of changes, you can look into "squashing" the commits.

    In GitKraken, this is done by selecting the multiple commits you want to combine, right clicking and selecting "Squash". There are some scenarios when squashing won't be available - in this case you might be best just leaving the history as-is!

    If I'm doing some DIY at home, things can get pretty messy with tools and dirt around the place. Before I go to bed, I might do a quick tidy up but the next day, once I've finished the piece of work, all the tools go away and I vacuum up the mess I made. I'll definitely have it clean and tidy before I let anybody else in the house! Treat your branches the same way. It's OK for them to be a bit messy while you've got things in progress, but as soon as you're finished or ready to merge into a shared branch, you ought to tidy it up - nobody wants to see your messy branch!

    One thing to note, though: don't squash your mistakes! Your mistakes and reworkings tell a story. It could provide valuable explanation to a future developer and documents your mistakes and learnings. Use amend and squash to tidy up, not to sweep under the rug!

    4. A branching Strategy is key

    There are many branching strategies out there and I don't really mind which one you use. My personal favourites are GitHub flow and Common Flow (or a combination of the two) but there are many more. Generally, these follow the feature-driven development pattern, with a branch for each feature and some notion of the state of a particular release (generally using branches or tags).

    I also like to ensure only one person is committing to any single branch at one time - if two people are working on a feature, break that feature down into sub-features. This is the only way my "messy work-in-progress branch" method works!

    GitHub flow, put very simply involves creating a branch for your feature (mapped to a GitHub issue), adding your commits to that branch and then opening a Pull Request. You discuss and review your code changes with your colleagues before deploying your changes for test. Once everyone is happy, your Pull Request is merged into the main branch. The GitHub flow tutorial goes into a little more detail.

    It's worth noting that if none of these git flows make sense to you, you can take inspiration from them. I don't know of many companies who strictly follow any one particular strategy. So pick one as a baseline and adapt it for your organisation.

    5. Rebase and merge - the best of both worlds!

    Alright, so you've got your branching strategy and you've completed your feature - we're ready to merge!

    But on larger projects with multiple concurrent workflows, you can end up with a real tangle of branches and merges.

    So what other options are there for merging these features?

    GitHub provides these 3 options on completion of a Pull Request:

    • Create a merge commit (which creates the mess you see above)
    • Squash and merge
    • Rebase and merge

    Now, some people like to squash and rebase: your entire feature gets squashed into one commit and plopped straight onto the develop or main branch -it's kind of an extreme alternative to GitHub's "squash and merge". This is very neat and tidy, I'll give you that! However, you lose a lot of the documentation of when, how and why code changes were made. I'd much rather keep this history where we can.

    Which leaves us with "Rebase and merge". This option allows us to avoid losing data while keeping the tree clean and tidy so is, of course, my favourite. (If you're using Azure DevOps, you can set this method as the merge type too - they call it a "semi-linear merge").

    Rebasing is the act of "replaying" each commit in a branch (our feature branch) on top of the target branch (main or develop in this case) as if they were happening at this newer point in time.

    As you can see, this method removes the muddle of criss-crossing merges, while maintaining the whole branch structure and all the detail in every commit. Each feature makes a neat "D" shape with the main or develop branch, with no crossovers.

    You can also enact this process without a Pull Request utilising the individual tools rebase your feature onto main and then merge your feature into main in any git client of your choosing.

    A note on rewriting history

    Both tips 4 and 5 do something controversial in the git world: rewriting history. Not "rewriting history" as in the act of whitewashing a historical atrocity, but simply changing what's been pushed to the git repository (AKA history) after its happened (rewriting). Squashing, amending, rebasing and patching are all examples of git history being rewritten.

    And I've just been actively advocating for 3 of these!

    Maintaining your "true history" certainly has its merits: it can be useful for learning and code reviews. It's also impossible to mess up your history if you never tamper with it!

    However, the way I see it is that this shouldn't at the cost of the readability of your repo. I prefer to maintain what I like to call my "feature history" where the order of our feature development is true, even if we're tweaking the timelines a little of when each feature was developed relative to each other.

    I don't advocate for complete rewriting, just a quick tidy up.

    As for that "risk" I mention when rewriting history, just make sure you've pushed your repository before you start messing with history. Then double check before your force push.

    From code dump to stunning archive of software

    And that's it. That's our stunning archive of software. We're source controlled with small incremental commits, each having a meaningful and detailed commit message sat comfortably in feature branches with no distracting noise and complicated merges. It reads easily from bottom to top, with no detail hidden or mistakes masked.

    A clean history allows for features to be rolled back easily, while detailed commit messages create a better understanding of a repositories history leading to fewer mistakes and a branching strategy can clarify what code is deployed to which environment at any point in time. It's now plain to see how git discipline can provide benefits to developers, managers and the end client - saving time, frustration and money for all involved in a project.
item.author: joeglombek

item.title: 善待群众 | Taking Friendly to the masses
item.description:
但是我们如何才能将这种友好性注入到我们的工作中，并使我们制作的网站尽可能地友好呢？

友好

以愉快、友善的方式对待某人

为了对所有人友好，我们需要确保每个人都可以使用我们的网站。 这个简单的概念是背后的全部前提，最初听起来令人生畏，可访问性。 与流行的看法相反，可访问性不仅仅与屏幕阅读器有关，还与您的网站对任何人的可用性有关，无论他们的个人需求如何。

可访问性

使接近、进入或使用它成为可能的事物的质量或特征

近几个月来，Umbraco 后台的可访问性已经开始形成：在任何新的 Umbraco 版本中，都有一个完整的版本说明专门用于改进可访问性。 这为越来越多的社区成员带来了可访问性的概念，但对一些人来说，一个可访问的网站似乎仍然是一个遥远的梦想——充其量是积压的低优先级票，最坏的情况是在规划阶段被驳回。

但我在这里挑战这一点，并向您展示构建一个更易于访问的网站如何比您想象的更容易，无论您是开发人员、设计师还是从事管理、销售和交付工作。

可访问性优先

当然，致力于让您的网站尽可能易于访问的时间和预算是最好的选择，这样做有很多理由，无论是在道德上还是在经济上。

我们认为网络是开放访问和公平的竞争环境，让每个人都可以访问相同的信息。 它是。 在大多数情况下。 建立可访问的网站是向更多人开放信息和服务的一种非常简单的方法，可确保人们不受其环境的不公平限制。

记住这些是客户也很重要。 就像店主不会在入口处设置障碍，只为那些可以跳过的人服务一样，网站为什么要为一定比例的潜在客户设置这些虚拟障碍？

我相信你们都知道去年在美国最高法院提起的多米诺披萨诉讼，但耐克、PornHub 和碧昂丝等其他大牌也被美国告上了法庭，其他国家也在追赶。 这只是用户体验

用户体验（对于那些知道的人来说是 UX）设计师以为用户设计出色的体验而自豪（我想线索就在名字中！），而不是简单地看起来不错的东西。

毫不奇怪，这种思维方式通常包括那些有访问问题的人。 正如“可访问性”这个词所暗示的那样，这种用户体验并不仅仅帮助那些有特定需求的人——它会帮助每个人。

在阳光明媚的日子里，任何使用闪亮手机屏幕的用户都会欣赏文字和背景之间的高对比度，而任何度过了疲惫一天的人都会欣赏简洁的语言和清晰的动作。 更不用说八分之一的色盲男性（包括我自己）以及我们中很大一部分人在以后的生活中会出现与运动、视力或听力相关的问题。

可访问性对某些人来说至关重要，对每个人都有用。 可访问性是 UX 的一部分，所以我们一定要接受它，并将其与视觉外观一样高度重视。

作为设计师，考虑用户如何与您的设计进行交互是工作的一部分，并且可访问性与您已经遵循的其他最佳实践完全吻合。 除了一些额外的事情需要考虑之外，这里没有什么额外的，而且您制作的设计越容易访问，它就越容易。

编码是关键

作为开发人员，我们对网站进行编码的方式会有所不同。 即使没有预留特殊预算，或者我们收到的设计没有充分考虑可访问性（或者根本没有设计！），我们仍然可以发挥作用。

这与不考虑可访问性的编码没有什么不同：就像我们通过编写有效的标记来支持浏览器一样，我们可以通过编写有效的语义标记来支持尽可能多的人。 开发人员可访问性的关键在于，实际上，我们编写的大多数 HTML 可能已经是可访问的。 为正确的事情使用正确的元素是一种很好的做法：所有导航区域的导航； 用于拆分页面的页眉、页脚和部分元素； 并正确使用标题* 。

麻烦来自 JavaScript 和 CSS。

在样式设置时，打破一些不良做法的一个很好的迹象是，如果您将样式设置为无或继承。 浏览器几乎总是有理由以某种方式设置某些样式，如果您要撤消它，它可能会使其更难使用。

可访问性提示

对于阅读本文的设计师、开发人员和测试人员，以下是我的一些重要提示，可帮助您开始无障碍之旅。 那里还有很多东西要学（ Jeffrey 和 Jorgen 的 2015 年的文章是一个很好的地方来扩展你的知识），但这是一个开始的好地方。

我有 3 条可访问样式的黄金法则。

大纲是浏览器显示焦点的默认方式 - 通过删除它，它可能会使您无法通过键盘导航您的网站！ 考虑更改轮廓颜色而不是完全删除它，或者使用其他一些清晰的样式来突出焦点。

通过从链接中删除颜色和下划线，您就不太清楚该文本实际上是一个链接！ 即使将链接从蓝色更改为任何其他颜色也会使导航对某些用户感到困惑。

如果你想让你的链接看起来像一个按钮，你可能会更加混淆人们。

将元素用于它们不是的东西是另一个危险信号。 使用链接作为按钮或按钮作为链接意味着用户无法从这种元素中获得他们期望的功能 - 链接将您导航到其他地方，而按钮与当前页面交互。 复选框是表单字段，而不是菜单的切换。

将元素用于不同的事物不一定是错误的，但它是一个警告信号。 因此，如果您是，您应该确保尝试将组件与屏幕阅读器一起使用（我建议在 Windows Narrator 上使用NVDA - 它是免费且非常受欢迎的）并且仅使用键盘导航。 如果某些事情不清楚或未按预期运行，请使用 ARIA 属性和 JavaScript 来纠正任何令人困惑的行为。

不要给我贴上愤世嫉俗的标签

从样式开始，使您的网站易于访问的另一个非常快速的技巧是标记事物。 如果输入没有标签，或者部分没有标题，请考虑添加一个“视觉隐藏”或通过 aria-label。 同样，如果您有一个没有文字的图标，通常值得添加一些文字以帮助人们理解其含义（它可能不像您想象的那么明显），但如果您的设计禁止这样做，请使用 aria-label ！

相反，没有语义含义的图标可以隐藏 aria-hidden，这样屏幕阅读器就不会读出它。 这方面的一个示例是旁边带有标签的图标 - 该图标不会为标签添加额外的含义，因此屏幕阅读器不需要读出它。

另类思维

图像上的替代文本（alt 属性）有几个非常方便的用途。 首先，如果您的图像由于某种原因无法加载，则会将其替换为 alt 文本，以了解它打算提供的上下文。 其次，替代文本可以为盲人用户提供图像的听觉描述，并为部分视力的用户提供更清晰的图像。

尽管每个部分使用一个 h1 是有效的 HTML，但一些屏幕阅读器（以及搜索引擎）很难理解这样的内容结构，因此仍然认为每页使用一个 h1 元素是最佳实践。

在不确定的结论中

这些只是开始您的无障碍之旅的一些提示。 我绝不是专家，这绝不是要知道的全部，但我热衷于让我的网站尽可能易于访问并每天学习更多，我希望我也能对你产生影响！ 如果您有任何无障碍查询或参与无障碍团队，请随时与我联系，我们都很友好。

瓦西里·波洛夫尼约夫—— Apr 25 4月25日



原文:

Umbraco is "The Friendly CMS" and, as a community, we aim to be warm and welcoming, greeting everyone we encounter with a Friendly high-five. But how can we inject some of this Friendliness into our work and make the websites we craft as Friendly as possible?

friendly

behaving in a pleasant, kind way towards someone

To be Friendly to everyone, we need to make sure everyone can use our websites. This simple concept is the whole premise behind, what may initially sound daunting, accessibility. Contrary to popular belief, accessibility isn't all about screen-readers, it's about the usability of your website to anyone, no matter their personal needs.

accessibility

the quality or characteristic of something that makes it possible to approach, enter, or use it

Accessibility of the Umbraco back-office has started to take shape over recent months: in any new Umbraco release, there’s been a whole section of the release notes dedicated to accessibility improvements. This has brought the concept of accessibility to more and more community members, but to some, an accessible website still seems like a distant pipedream - at best a low priority ticket on a backlog and at worst dismissed in the planning stages.

But I’m here to challenge that and show you how building a more accessible site is easier than you might think, whether you're a developer, designer or working in management, sales and delivery.

Accessibility as a priority

Of course, time and budget dedicated to making your website as accessible as possible is the best option and there are many reasons to do so, morally and financially.

We think of the web as open-access and a level playing field giving everyone access to the same information. And it is. For the most part. Building accessible websites is a very simple way to open up information and services to more people, ensuring people are not unfairly restricted by their circumstances.

It’s also important to remember that these are customers. Just as a shopkeeper wouldn’t put a barrier across their entrance only serving those who could jump it, why should a website put up these virtual hurdles for a percentage of potential customers?

I’m sure you’re all aware of the Domino's Pizza lawsuit that hit the US Supreme Court last year, but other big names like Nike, PornHub and Beyonce also got taken to court in America with other countries catching on. Web accessibility will soon be a must-have for all big corporations and any website built today should be taking this seriously.

It’s just UX

User experience (that’s UX to those in the know) designers pride themselves in designing great experiences for users (I guess the clue is in the name!) rather than something that simply looks good.

No surprises here, that this way of thinking generally includes those with access issues. As the word “accessibility” implies, this kind of UX doesn’t exclusively help those with specific needs - it’ll help everyone.

Any user of a shiny phone screen on a bright sunny day will appreciate high contrast ratios between text and the background, while any person who’s had an exhausting day will appreciate concise language and clear actions. Not to mention the 1 in 8 men who are colourblind (myself included) and the huge proportion of us who will develop motor, sight or hearing-related issues in later life.

Accessibility is vital for some and useful for everyone. Accessibility is a part of UX, so let’s be sure to embrace it and hold it in as high regard as visual appearance.

As a designer, thinking about how a user might interact with your design is a part of the job and accessibility fits right in with the other best practices you’re already following. There’s nothing extra here except a few additional things to think about and the more accessible designs you produce, the easier it becomes.

Coding is key

As developers, the way we code our sites makes all the difference. Even if there's no special budget set aside or we've received a design that hasn't fully considered accessibility (or there's no design at all!), we can still make all the difference.

It's not that different from coding without considering accessibility: just as we support browsers by writing valid markup, we can support as many people as possible by writing valid, semantic markup. The key to accessibility for developers is that actually, most HTML we write is probably accessible already. HTML renders in an easy to read and understand way and if we’re using the right semantic markup, it goes a long way to being understood by screen-readers too. It’s good practice to use the right elements for the right things: a nav for all navigation areas; main for the primary content of your site; header, footer and section elements for splitting up the page; and by using headings correctly*.

The trouble comes with JavaScript and CSS.

When styling, a good indication of breaking some bad practice is if you set a style to none or inherit. Browsers almost always have a reason for styling something a certain way, and if you’re undoing that it could make it harder to use.

Accessible accessibility tips

For you designers, developers and testers reading this, here are some of my top tips for starting on your accessibility journey. There is a lot more to learn out there (Jeffrey and Jorgen's article from 2015 is a good place to look for expanding your knowledge next) but this is a good place to get started.

I have 3 golden rules for styling accessibly.

1. Let me outline this for you

An outline is the browsers’ default way of showing focus - by removing this, it might make navigating your site by keyboard impossible! Consider changing the outline colour rather than removing it completely, or use some other clear styling to highlight focus.

2. Which links nicely onto...

By removing the colour and underline from a link, you make it less clear that the text is actually a link! Even changing a link from blue to any other colour can make navigation confusing to some users.

If you’re trying to make your link look like a button, you might confuse people even more.

3. Things that look like things they are not

Using elements for things they are not is another red flag. Using a link as a button or button as a link means the user doesn’t get the functionality they expect from that kind of element - links navigate you to somewhere else, while a button interacts with the current page. A checkbox is a form field, not a toggle for a menu.

It’s not necessarily wrong to use an element for something different, but it is a warning sign. So if you are, you should be sure to try using the component with a screen-reader (I recommend using NVDA over Windows Narrator - it's free and very popular) and only using keyboard navigation. If something is unclear or doesn't function as expected, use ARIA attributes and JavaScript to correct any confusing behaviour.

Don't label me a cynic

Moving on from styling, another really quick tip to make your site accessible is to label things. If an input has no label, or a section has no heading, consider adding one that's “visually hidden” or through aria-label. Similarly, if you have an icon with no text, it’s generally worth adding some text to help people understand its meaning (it might not be as obvious as you think), but if your design prohibits this, aria-label it!

Conversely, an icon that has no semantic meaning, can be aria-hidden so that it’s not read out by a screen-reader. An example of this would be an icon with a label next to it - the icon adds no additional meaning to the label, so a screen-reader doesn’t need to read it out.

Alternative thinking

Alternative text (alt attribute) on images have a couple of really handy uses. Firstly, if your image can't be loaded for whatever reason, it is replaced by the alt text to give an idea of the context it was meant to provide. Secondly, alt text can provide an audible description of the image for blind users and more clarity to a partially sighted user.

*A note on headings

Although it’s valid HTML to use an h1 per section, some screen-readers (as well as search engines) struggle to understand the structure of the content like this so it’s still considered best practice to use one h1 element per page.

In inconclusive conclusion

These are just a few pointers to start you on your accessibility journey. I'm by no means an expert and this is by no means all there is to know but I am keen to make my sites as accessible as possible and learning more every day, which I hope I have managed to rub off on you too! Don't hesitate to reach out to me if you have any accessibility queries or get involved with the Accessibility Team, we're all quite Friendly.

Vasily Polovnyov - Apr 25
item.author: joeglombek

item.title: 在 Web 开发项目中选择正确的技术 | Choosing the right technologies on a web development project
item.description:
让我们陈述一个假设的用例，您被聘为一家初创公司的首席技术官，该公司拥有用于特定目的的产品（网络平台）（这里的细节并不重要）。

该公司几个月前就将其产品投放市场并从中获得收益，但他们希望创建一种新产品以与现有产品集成，并要求您进行预览分析并选择哪些技术（语言、架构、平台等）你应该使用给定的新产品。

决策


事实上，要使用的技术不是“你/他们最喜欢什么”的决定，你必须用预览分析来支持你的决定，其中必须包含一些我将尝试总结的要点：

我们假设您已经聘请了一些开发人员，因此您必须牢记他们使用不同语言的经验、他们为不同架构开发的经验以及他们的整体开发经验。

也与他们交谈并了解他们的意见。 有些人不想改变。 如果一个人花了 20 年时间使用 Java 或 .Net 环境进行开发，他/她可能不想去做不同的事情并学习不同的工作方式，如果您的团队是这种情况，请让他们做出决定.

如果您的所有团队都同意包括新的/不同的技术，那没关系，但如果不是，请记住这一点。

您还可以让不喜欢更改的人维护旧软件，而其他人则开发新软件。

这意味着您需要在公司办公室附近了解这项技术的人。

毕竟，您将需要雇用一些开发人员，并且您身边没有知道这项技术的人可能会使招聘过程变得更加困难或无法雇用高级开发人员。 仅与初级开发人员一起开始一个项目是没有公司出于明显原因而希望成为的情况。

我很久以前就开始起草这篇文章了，现在我们已经接受了远程作为公司提供的一个很好的选择，这是必须的。 但是有些人不喜欢远程，或者他们只喜欢每周在办公室社交一两次，所以上一段还是有道理的。

你有没有问过为什么你在家里尝试的一些技术没有很快被广泛接受？

我记得在有和没有 Sapper 的情况下尝试过 Svelte.js 并想哇我真的很喜欢这个：基于目录的自动路由，vanilla js (ES6)，很好的热重载......

然后我们在我工作的公司开始了一个项目，最后我们在接下来的两个不同需求的项目中使用了 Angular 和 React。

为什么？ 当有一种语言/框架没有被广泛使用时，很难找到有经验的开发人员。
这也是一个不好的信号，因为该技术可能会停产，然后您将有两种选择：分叉项目并自己维护它或将项目重构为不同的语言/框架。

这两种选择都很昂贵，您可以通过使用由大公司（或非常大的社区）支持并在世界范围内广泛使用的东西来避免这种情况。

就是这样，降低风险。

每年都有大量（非常大量）的框架、实用程序、编译器、语言等在 git 存储库上向公众推出，并带有很好的登录页面，并承诺解决实际需求。

语言和框架出现的原因不同：可扩展性成本、开发成本、追溯兼容性、托管成本、可维护性成本……

这一切都是为了减少实现目标所需的成本，以便自由职业者和使用这些工具的公司可以降低总体成本。

您需要分析每个细节以找到最适合您的项目的细节。

关于所有这些点的错误决定有时也被称为过度工程。 那是因为使用 java 和 sql server 来提升静态登录页面是一种财务自杀。 当您可以简单地使用静态 HTML 对其进行编码时，您为什么要支付在这种条件下保持 Java 应用程序运行的最低要求（服务器的最小 RAM，您的 APP 的另一个 RAM，更高的核心数、磁盘空间、节点等）和 CSS/Sass，添加表单验证所需的更少的 javascript，使用 Parcel.js 之类的打包器将其全部打包，仅此而已。

您可以在主机上使用低核心数、低内存数和小于 300kb 的重量，而且您将获得 SEO 和 Core Web Vitals 的好处。

另一方面，如果制作银行应用程序只是作为一个额外的例子，我可能会选择 Java 或 C#。

这些是我们作为开发人员不介意的 4 个关键点，但在管理项目或管理公司时要牢记这一点很重要。 您还处理了哪些其他关键点？



原文:


Lets state an hypothetical use case where you are hired as CTO on a startup that has a product (web platform) for a given purpose (the details here are not important).

The company has its product on the market since some months ago and getting earnings from that but they want to create a new product to integrate with the already existing one and asks you to make a preview analysis and choose which technologies (language, architecture, platform etc) should you use for that given new product.

Decision Making


In fact, the technologies to use are not a decision on "what you/they like most", you have to back your decision with that preview analysis which must contain some points that I'll try to summarize:

1- Developers experience

We assume you already have some developers hired so you have to keep in mind them experience with different languages, their experience developing for different architectures and their overall experience developing at all.

Also speak with them and know their opinion. There are people that don't want to change. If a person spent 20 years developing with Java or in a .Net environment, he/she may not want to go for a different thing and learn a different way to work, if it's the case in your team, ask them to make a decision.

If all your team agrees with including new/different technologies it's OK, but if not, take it in mind.

You also can set people that don't like changes to maintain old software while others develop the new one.

2- Knowledge proximity

This means that you need people knowing this technologies close to your companies office.

You will need to hire some developers after all and not having people next to you that know this technologies could make the process of hiring much harder or making it impossible to hire senior devs. Starting a project with junior developers only is a situation where no company wants to be for obvious reasons.

I started the draft of this post long time ago and now we have accepted REMOTE as a good option to offer in companies, a must. But there are people that don't like remote or they just like to socialise once or twice a week at the office, so the previous paragraph still makes some sense.

3- Usage

Haven't you asked why some technologies you try at home are not widely accepted quickly?

I remember trying Svelte.js with and without Sapper and thinking wow I'm really enjoying this: Automagically routing directory based, vanilla js (ES6), nice hot reloading...

Then we started a project on the company I work in and we ended using Angular and React on the next two projects with different needs.

Why? When there's a language/framework that it's not widely used it's difficult to find developers with experience on it.
Also It's a bad signal because the technology could be discontinued and then you will have two options: Fork the project and maintaining it by yourself or refactor the project into a different language/framework.

Both options are expensive and you can avoid this by just using something backed by a big company (or a very big community) and widely used around the world.

That's just it, reducing the risk.

4- knowledge of the reason to be for these technologies

Every year a big (very big) number of frameworks, utilities, compilers, languages and so roll out to the public on git repositories, with nice landing pages along the promise of solving a real need.

Languages and frameworks appear for different reasons: cost of scalability, cost of development, retro-compatibility, hosting costs, cost of maintainability...

It's all about reducing what it takes to reach something so freelancers and companies using those tools can reduce in costs overall.

You need to analyse the details of each to find which fits best for your project.

The bad decisions about all those points are sometimes called OverEngineering too. That's because using java and sql server for raising a static landing page it's a financial suicide. Why would you want to pay the minimum requirements for keeping your java app up on this conditions (minimum RAM for the server, another for your APP, higher core counts, disk space, nodes and so) when you can simply code it with static HTML and CSS/Sass, add the less javascript you need for form validation, wrap it all using a bundler like Parcel.js and that's all.

You will be ok with a low core count, low ram count and less than 300kb weight on your hosting plus you'll get benefits on SEO and Core Web Vitals.

By the other hand I'll probably go for Java or C# if making a bank app just as an extra example.

Those are the 4 key points we don't mind as devs but that are important to take in mind when you manage a project or rule a company. What other key points do you handle?
item.author: joelbonetr

item.title: Array.every() - 用于检查所有项目是否满足条件 | Array.every() - for checking if all items meet a condition
item.description:
本文是阵列方法系列的第八篇。 在本文中，我将解释every Array 方法是什么。

数组的every方法是一个高阶函数，它断言数组中的所有项是否满足特定条件。 如果所有项目都满足，则返回true ，如果至少一项不满足，则返回false 。

此方法不修改数组。 它只循环并在每个项目上应用一个条件，直到找到不匹配的那个。 这意味着如果它找到一个不匹配的元素，它不会继续循环遍历数组中的剩余项。 它立即返回false 。 如果它在整个循环中都没有找到这样的项目，那么它返回true

传递给every方法的callbackFunction应用于数组中的每一项，直到找到与函数中的条件不匹配的项。

在每个循环中传递给回调函数的参数是item 、项目的index和原始array 。

没有every方法

这是一个模仿every方法的例子：

 const array = [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 , 8 ] let allNumbersLessThan5 = false ; for ( let i = 0 ; i < array . length ; i ++ ) { const item = array [ i ] console . log ( item ) if ( item >= 5 ) { allNumbersLessThan5 = false break } } console . log ( allNumbersLessThan5 ) // 1 // 2 // 3 // 4 // 5 // false

这种循环方法类似于every方法在后台执行的操作。 它循环遍历每个项目，当它找到不符合指定条件的项目（所有数字都应该小于 5 ）时，它会停止循环并返回false 。

从日志中，您会看到循环在5处停止，并且由于5没有通过测试，因此该方法已经知道不是每个项目都满足条件。

如果它没有找到停止循环的理由，那么这意味着所有的数字都小于 5。

使用 Every 方法

以下是使用every获得先前结果的方法：

 const array = [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 , 8 ] const allNumbersLessThan5 = array . every ( item => { console . log ( item ) return item < 5 }) console . log ( allNumbersLessThan5 ) // 1 // 2 // 3 // 4 // 5 // false

用更少的代码行更容易阅读。 让我们看一个一切都通过的例子：

 const array = [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 , 8 ] const allNumbersLessThan5 = array . every ( item => { return typeof item === " number " }) console . log ( allNumbersLessThan5 ) // true

每个项目都通过条件，所以确实，每个项目都是一个数字。

当数组中有不同的值并且想要断言它们都满足条件时， every方法很有用。



原文:

This article is the eighth of the Array Method Series. In this article, I will explain what the every Array method is.

What is the Every Method?

The every method of arrays is a higher-order function that asserts if ALL items in an array meet a certain condition. If all items meet it, it returns true, and if at least one item does not meet it, it returns false.

This method does not modify the array. It only loops through and applies a condition on each item until it finds the one that does not match. This means that if it finds an element that does not match, it doesn't continue looping through the remaining items in the array. It immediately returns false. And if it never finds such an item all through the loop, then it returns true

Syntax of the Every Method 

array.every(function(item, index, array){ // condition to test item with // return true or false
})

The callbackFunction passed to the every method is applied to each item in the array until it finds the item that does not match the condition in the function.

The arguments passed to the callback function in each loop are the item, the index of the item, and the original array.

Without the Every Method

The every method is an abstracted function that does a quick check and stops at the first item that does not pass a certain criterion. Here's an example imitating the every method:

const array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8] let allNumbersLessThan5 = false; for (let i = 0; i < array.length; i++) { const item = array[i] console.log(item) if (item >= 5) { allNumbersLessThan5 = false break }
} console.log(allNumbersLessThan5)
// 1
// 2
// 3
// 4
// 5
// false

This loop approach is similar to what the every method does in the background. It loops through each item, and when it finds the item that does not match the specified condition (that all numbers should be less than 5), it stops the loop and returns false.

From the logs, you would see that the loop stopped at 5, and since 5 did not pass the test, then the method already knows that NOT EVERY item meets the condition.

If it doesn't find a reason to stop the loop, then it means that all the numbers are less than 5.

With the Every Method

Here's how you achieve the previous result with every:

const array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8] const allNumbersLessThan5 = array.every(item => { console.log(item) return item < 5
}) console.log(allNumbersLessThan5)
// 1
// 2
// 3
// 4
// 5
// false

Easier to read with fewer lines of code. Let's see an example where everything passes:

const array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8] const allNumbersLessThan5 = array.every(item => { return typeof item === "number"
}) console.log(allNumbersLessThan5)
// true

Every item passes the condition, so indeed, EVERY item is a number.

The every method is useful when you have different values in an array and you want to assert that all of them meet a condition.

item.title: Dev.to 越来越危险！ | Dev.to is getting dangerous!
item.description:
当我看到初学者试图通过教学来学习时，我通常会支持（至少我希望如此）。 写一篇文章来帮助自己理解你认为你知道的东西并没有错。

但最近，它变得几乎危险了。 就在今天，我看到了可能非常有害的代码，这些代码可能会弄乱和删除您的文件。 在评论中，我看到一个无知的家伙想使用此代码来组织他的照片。

更糟糕的是什么？ 这篇文章的作者不仅没有警告那个人不要使用他的代码，甚至鼓励他这样做，可能是因为他觉得被承认了。

因此，如果您是这里或其他任何地方的初学者，在尝试其他人的代码时要非常谨慎，尤其是在涉及文件系统时。 永远不要复制和运行代码，即使你认为你理解它。

潜在的危险很难被发现。 在这种特殊情况下，我怀疑代码在重新运行后会删除文件。 不是全部，只有少数。 如果我不尝试以更花哨的方式重新创建程序，我自己不会看到它。

通常，不会导致副作用的编程任务不应危及您的数据。 但问题是初学者可能无法识别潜在的副作用，也无法理解副作用是什么。

我必须重复一遍：保持警惕。 我看到一个讨论，其中一个白痴就如何修复 Linux 生产服务器向新手提供了“有价值的建议”：

不要这样做！ 它可能会删除您的所有数据。 你可以在这里阅读更多

如果您很好奇，新手认为这是认真的建议并做到了！ 所谓的专家为自己辩解，说他不相信有人会这么傻！



原文:

I am usually supportive (at least I hope so) when I see beginners trying to learn by teaching. There is nothing wrong with writing an article to help yourself understand what you think you know.

But lately, it is getting almost dangerous. Just today, I saw potentially very harmful code that can mess and delete your files. In the comments, I saw a clueless guy wanting to use this code to organize his photos.

And what is worse? The author of the article not only did not warn that guy about not using his code but even encouraged him to do so, probably because he felt acknowledged.

So if you are a beginner here or anywhere else, be very cautious when trying someone else's code, especially when a filesystem is involved. Never copy and never run the code, even if you think you understand it.

The potential danger can be hard to spot. In this particular case, I suspect the code deletes files after re-run. Not all of them, just a few. I would not see it myself if I did not try to re-create the program in a more fancy way.

In general, programming tasks that cause no side effects should not endanger your data. But the problem is that beginners would probably not be able to identify potential side effects nor understand what a side effect is.

I have to repeat: stay alert. I saw a discussion in which an idiot offered a "valuable piece of advice" to a newbie on how to fix a Linux production server:

Don't do this! It may delete all your data. You can read more here

If you are curious, the newbie thought it was serious advice and did it! The so-called expert defended himself, saying he didn't believe someone could have been so stupid to do it!

 head () { return { title : " My Page " , script : [{ type : ' application/ld+json ' , innerHTML : JSON . stringify ( this . jsonld ) // <- set jsonld object in data or wherever you want }], __dangerouslyDisableSanitizers : [ ' script ' ], // <- this is important }; },

 head() { return { title: "My Page", script: [{ type: 'application/ld+json', innerHTML: JSON.stringify(this.jsonld) // <- set jsonld object in data or wherever you want }], __dangerouslyDisableSanitizers: ['script'], // <- this is important }; },

item.description:
基里尔·阿诺欣

发表于您是否有效地使用您的代码编辑器？
你的代码既快速又优雅吗？

由于代码编辑器是每个开发人员的主要工具，因此至关重要的是要完美地掌握它。 这将使您提高工作效率并从编码中获得真正的乐趣。 这就是键盘快捷键可以提供帮助的地方！

查看Hotkeys.guru并参加调查！ 它包括 48 个示例，说明您可以（并且应该！）在编辑器中做什么：快速导航、有效修改、全面探索代码等。

免责声明：

  1. 您使用哪个编辑器并不重要。 所有流行的编辑器都具有调查中提到的功能，或者至少具有相同的功能。 我已经使用WebStorm来准备这些示例。
  2. 据推测，您已经使用箭头移动插入符号并使用Ctrl+X ( Cmd+X )、 Ctrl+C ( Cmd+C )、 Ctrl+V ( Cmd+V ) 来剪切、复制和粘贴。

任何反馈都非常感谢！

Do you use your code editor efficiently?
Do you code fast and elegant?

As a code editor is the main tool of every developer, the crucial thing is to master it perfectly. This will allow you to boost your productivity and get real pleasure from coding. And that's where keyboard shortcuts can help!

Check out Hotkeys.guru and take the survey! It includes 48 illustrated examples of what you can (and should!) do in your editor: quick navigation, effective modification, comprehensive exploration of your code, etc.

Disclaimer: 

  1. It doesn't matter which editor you use. All popular editors have functionality mentioned in the survey or at least something equal. I have used WebStorm to prepare these examples.
  2. Presumably, you already use arrows to move a caret and Ctrl+X (Cmd+X), Ctrl+C (Cmd+C), Ctrl+V (Cmd+V) to cut, copy and paste.

Any feedback is highly appreciated!
